story:
O’Brien...
A name I kept hearing in childhood dreams, a name without a face or story. Only the name.
...O’Brien.

I was at work when I saw her. Clear blue eyes. In the instant our eyes locked, a vision struck me—real, bright, whole. I saw our life: our first kiss; our first fight and the making up; moving in together; the first time we made love; vows; the hundredth kiss. Children—two boys and a girl: Toby, Ryan, Eliza. Years rippling forward, her hand in mine as we aged; our grandchildren; her lips on my forehead as I drifted away. All of it in a single heartbeat.

And in the present, she couldn’t breathe. She’d been hit by a car. I was the paramedic. We lifted her into the ambulance. I didn’t want to let go of her hand, and she didn’t look away from me—until my partner shouted, “Clear!” and I had to break contact while he shocked her chest. The world inside the vision kept blooming—until I noticed someone else in the ambulance. Someone who didn’t belong.

A man with ash-gray smoke curling off him. Scaly wings. He grinned at me. A small light, bright and trembling like a breath caught in glass, floated from her open mouth—her cry trapped inside it. He plucked it from the air into his ashy palm. “I’ll take that,” he whispered, and smiled.

“You should’ve gone on that date they arranged for you.”

“GIVE HER BACK!” I shouted.

He clicked his tongue. “Give what back? It was only an alteration of a time that never would have happened.” He faded, blowing away like dust.

The ambulance doors flew open. We rolled her into the hospital.

“Name of patient?” someone called.

“Kaley O’Brien,” my partner said.

The name hit me like a fist. O’Brien... the dreams. Dreams I never remembered except for the name. I tugged at it, and the dark gave way: the world burning; cities swallowed; a throne of bones; a woman seated there, calm and terrible. Her.

I stared at Kaley’s still face. Even as the doctors worked, I knew it was too late. I kept holding her hand. I had already held that hand through a lifetime that didn’t exist.

“O’Brien...” I breathed, tasting the fear of knowing too much. I had seen the life we might have had—a vision drenched in warmth and ordinary love. But in my youth, I had dreamed of endings and ruin, and of her at the center of it.

A vision is not a dream. One shows what could be. The other warns what may come. Sometimes they trade places. Sometimes they change—by choice, by chance, by the dates we skip, by the shadows that slip into our ambulances and smile while we beg.

I thought of what he said: You should’ve gone on that date they arranged. Maybe if I had, the vision would have become our life. Maybe it would have ended the world. Maybe both truths were waiting—one in light, one in smoke—and the smallest difference decided which door opened.

Kaley O’Brien. A name I had always known. A future that never happened. A dream that might yet be.
